_work.title: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
_work.creator: various
_work.place: Egypt, Eastern Mediterranean, Languedoc & the Balkans
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: Images of Gnostic scripture and cosmology in depth: the Pistis Sophia and the Bruce Codex (Books of Jeu) ritual diagrams, the reconstructed Ophite serpent-diagram, the lion-headed demiurge Ialdabaoth, the Abraxas/Abrasax anguipede and Chnoubis magical gems, the Apocryphon of John, the Holy Wisdom (Sophia) icons, and the medieval dualist afterlives of Gnosticism — the Cathars of Languedoc and the Bogomils of Bosnia.

entries.title: *Pistis Sophia* — page of the Coptic Gnostic gospel (modern edition)
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Anonymous (3rd-c. text); modern translated edition
entries.date: text 3rd–4th c. CE; this edition modern
entries.repository: British Library, London (Askew Codex, the underlying manuscript)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Elizeu Antônio de Souza). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A text-page of the *Pistis Sophia*, the long Coptic Gnostic dialogue preserved in the Askew Codex (British Library Add MS 5114) and here shown in a modern translation from the Coptic.

## Iconography
The image is a page of running text rather than a picture: the *Pistis Sophia* is unillustrated, its drama carried entirely in words. It recounts the risen Jesus instructing his disciples on the descent and repentance of the Aeon Pistis Sophia ("Faith-Wisdom").

## Significance
The *Pistis Sophia* narrates the fall of [[motif:sophia and the aeons|Sophia]] into the chaos below the thirteenth Aeon, her thirteen *Repentances*, and her rescue by the light — a sustained myth of error, suffering, and restored [[motif:gnosis|gnosis]]. The Askew Codex was acquired by the British Museum in 1785 and made famous by the editions of Schwartze, Carl Schmidt, and later Violet MacDermot. It is one of the very few complete Gnostic scriptures known before the Nag Hammadi find.

## For artists & game designers
A template for a 'lamentation' quest: a fallen light-being voices thirteen ascending pleas, each one a stage the player must complete to lift her back to the Pleroma.

entries.title: Gnostic Cross from the Bruce Codex (Books of Jeu)
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Coptic scribe)
entries.date: text 3rd–4th c. CE; codex later copy
entries.summary:
A cruciform ritual diagram copied from the *Bruce Codex*, the papyrus book (Bodleian MS Bruce 96) that preserves the two *Books of Jeu*.

## Iconography
The figure is a stark cross-shaped seal of the kind that punctuates the *Books of Jeu*: geometric arms enclosing letters, numbers, and divine names. Such diagrams were not decorative — they are the working apparatus of the text, the seals and passwords the ascending soul must present at each celestial gate.

## Significance
The *Books of Jeu* are among the earliest Gnostic ritual manuals to survive, describing how the soul rises through the *treasuries of light* by uttering the right name and displaying the right [[motif:seal|seal]] at each archon-guarded threshold. Carl Schmidt's edition established their importance; the diagrams are direct ancestors of the magical seals of the later grimoire tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A gate-key system in miniature: each diagram is a sigil-and-password the player must possess to pass a guardian and climb to the next sphere.

citations.text: Carl Schmidt & Violet MacDermot, *The Books of Jeu and the Untitled Text in the Bruce Codex* (1978).

entries.title: Magical Diagram from the *Books of Jeu*
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Coptic scribe)
entries.date: text 3rd–4th c. CE; codex later copy
entries.summary:
One of the *topos*-diagrams of the aeons from the *Books of Jeu* in the Bruce Codex — a famous early Gnostic ritual diagram.

## Iconography
A boxed emanation-diagram in which a central enclosure is ringed by smaller cells, each inscribed with letters, numerals, and the magical names of the powers it contains. The *Books of Jeu* are densely filled with such schematics, mapping the topography of the heavenly *treasuries* and the figure of Jeu, the 'true God' set as overseer over them.

## Significance
These diagrams are among the oldest surviving worked examples of Gnostic ritual cartography: they fix in ink the invisible architecture of the upper world so the initiate can navigate it. Each enclosed name is a station, each seal a key. Scholars from Carl Schmidt onward have treated them as a bridge between speculative cosmology and operative magic — the cosmos rendered as a diagram you can actually use.

## For artists & game designers
A literal level-map: nested chambers labelled with passwords, ready to drop in as the dungeon schematic of a celestial ascent.

citations.text: Carl Schmidt & Violet MacDermot, *The Books of Jeu and the Untitled Text in the Bruce Codex* (1978).

entries.title: The Ophite Diagram (reconstruction by Jacques Matter)
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Jacques Matter (19th-c. reconstruction)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: reproduced in Jacques Matter, Histoire critique du gnosticisme]
entries.summary:
A nineteenth-century reconstruction by Jacques Matter of the *Ophite Diagram*, the serpent-cosmos that Celsus described and Origen rebutted in *Contra Celsum*.

## Iconography
Concentric circles map the cosmos: an outer serpent (Leviathan as the encircling world-soul) rings a stack of lettered spheres, gated by named barriers and flaming swords. The seven planetary [[motif:ialdabaoth|archons]] occupy their orbits, and the soul's route upward is marked by the passwords needed to pass each guardian.

## Significance
The Ophite Diagram is our best window onto a lost Gnostic ritual chart, known only because Celsus mocked it and Origen quoted him to refute it around 248 CE. It schematises the dualist conviction that the visible cosmos is a prison of [[motif:seven archons|hostile powers]], escape from which requires secret knowledge. The diagram has been studied and re-drawn repeatedly since; Matter's version is among the most reproduced.

## For artists & game designers
The original 'cosmic prison map': a coiled serpent enclosing seven gated rings, each with a named gatekeeper and a passphrase — a complete ascent minigame on a single sheet.

entries.title: Lion-headed Aion (leontocephaline) — drawing of the Mithraic relief
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Unknown (19th-c. drawing, perhaps Félix Lajard)
entries.repository: Musée des Beaux-Arts et d'Archéologie, Vienne (relief R 2001-5-131)
entries.summary:
A nineteenth-century drawing of the Mithraic lion-headed god (*leontocephaline*) from a relief at Vienne — the iconography most often invoked to picture the Gnostic demiurge Ialdabaoth.

## Iconography
A standing winged figure with a lion's head, its body wound by a serpent whose coils rise to rest its head on the lion's crown. The god holds keys and a sceptre and is flanked by signs of the zodiac. In the Mithraic context the figure personifies boundless Time (Aion/Zervan); the same lion-and-serpent form was read by Gnostics as the face of the arrogant world-creator.

## Significance
Gnostic texts describe [[motif:ialdabaoth|Ialdabaoth]] / Yaldabaoth — the demiurge born of Sophia's error — as a lion-faced serpent who falsely proclaims himself sole god. Magical gems and the Mithraic leontocephaline supplied the visual vocabulary for this [[motif:demiurge|demiurge]]. Howard Jackson's study traces the lion-headed god across these traditions, showing how a single uncanny hybrid bridged mystery cult and Gnostic myth.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made false-god boss: lion-headed, serpent-wound, keys in hand, mid-roar — the tyrant who believes he is the only god.

citations.text: Howard M. Jackson, *The Lion Becomes Man* (1985).

entries.title: Roman Abrasax intaglio — cock-headed anguipede in bloodstone
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman-era gem-cutter)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Maxime Cambreling). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Roman magical intaglio in bloodstone (jasper) carved with *Abrasax* the anguipede — a cock-headed god with serpent legs — surrounded by the names of angels.

## Iconography
The figure stands in a cuirass, a cock's (or lion's) head above, two snakes for legs, brandishing a whip in one hand and a round shield in the other; the shield and field carry voces magicae and angelic names. This *alectorocephalic anguipede* is the single most recognisable type among the thousands of surviving 'Gnostic' or 'Abraxas' gems.

## Significance
The name [[motif:abraxas|Abrasax / Abraxas]] sums in Greek isopsephy to 365, the number of heavens and of days, marking the god as ruler of the cosmic year. Though the gems are products of a broad Graeco-Egyptian magical milieu rather than a single sect, Basilides' followers and later writers tied the name to Gnostic speculation, and Bonner's *Studies in Magical Amulets* remains the foundational catalogue.

## For artists & game designers
The quintessential occult-amulet asset: a snake-legged, beast-headed warrior with shield and whip, encircled by barbarous names — a self-contained sigil-item.

entries.title: Abraxas gems with human forms, from Macarius's *Abraxas seu Apistopistus*
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Jacob van Werden (engraver), after Jean Chifflet / Macarius
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: plate from Macarius, Abraxas seu Apistopistus, 1657]
entries.summary:
An engraved plate of Abraxas gems 'with human forms', from Jean l'Heureux's (Macarius's) *Abraxas, seu Apistopistus*, edited by Jean Chifflet (Antwerp, 1657) — the first great printed catalogue of the Gnostic amulet stones.

## Iconography
Rows of numbered gems are reproduced in fine engraving, each showing a deity or hybrid in human form with attendant inscriptions. The plate is an antiquarian inventory: object after object lifted onto the page so the learned reader can compare the magical types.

## Significance
Macarius's *Apistopistus* ('the unbelievable believed') gathered and engraved the [[motif:abraxas|Abraxas]]-stones at a moment when antiquaries were first treating magical gems as evidence rather than mere curiosities. Its plates fixed the visual canon of 'Gnostic gems' for two centuries and stand behind every later corpus down to Bonner and the modern *Campbell Bonner Magical Gems Database*.

## For artists & game designers
A period 'catalogue sheet' device: an array of numbered amulet thumbnails, perfect as an in-game compendium or codex page the player fills in by collecting gems.

entries.title: Chnoubis intaglio — lion-headed serpent magical gem (BnF)
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Graeco-Egyptian gem-cutter)
entries.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Paris (Cabinet des Médailles)
entries.summary:
A magical intaglio in the Bibliothèque nationale de France (Cabinet des Médailles) carved with *Chnoubis* — a lion-headed serpent crowned with solar rays.

## Iconography
The gem shows an upright serpent with a radiate lion's head, often accompanied by the triple-bar 'Chnoubis sign' and barbarous names. The type is compact and emblematic, the whole power of the stone concentrated in one hybrid creature.

## Significance
Chnoubis was an *agathodaimon* (good spirit) and solar-stomach guardian: such gems were worn as healing amulets, especially against ailments of the stomach and digestion, drawing on the serpent's chthonic and the lion's solar force. Like the [[motif:abraxas|Abraxas]] stones, Chnoubis gems sit at the crossroads of Graeco-Egyptian magic and the [[motif:demiurge|lion-serpent]] imagery that Gnostic writers read into the figure of the archon. They are catalogued by Bonner and in the Campbell Bonner database.

## For artists & game designers
A tidy talisman-icon: radiant lion-headed snake on a single stone — drop-in art for a healing charm or status-cure item.

entries.title: Gnostic gem with scarab (Walters Art Museum)
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Graeco-Egyptian gem-cutter)
entries.summary:
A so-called 'Gnostic gem' in the Walters Art Museum, carved with a scarab — an Egyptian symbol of rebirth absorbed into the magical-amulet repertoire of late antiquity.

## Iconography
The small engraved stone presents a scarab beetle, the Egyptian *kheper*, set among magical signs. As an amulet it folds the Pharaonic emblem of self-renewal into the Graeco-Roman magical idiom that museums conventionally label 'Gnostic'.

## Significance
The Walters' label 'Gnostic gem' reflects the older catalogue habit of grouping all the engraved Graeco-Egyptian magical stones under that heading; modern scholarship treats them as products of a broad ritual culture rather than of any one Gnostic church. The scarab's presence shows how deeply [[motif:abraxas|these amulets]] drew on Egyptian funerary symbolism of death and [[motif:demiurge|rebirth]], a continuity studied by Bonner, Michel, and the Campbell Bonner database.

## For artists & game designers
A museum-real amulet asset: a carved beetle-stone for a 'rebirth' or 'second chance' charm, with authentic Greco-Egyptian provenance.

entries.title: *Apocryphon of John* — the Sethian creation myth (text page)
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Coptic scribe)
entries.date: text 2nd c. CE; Coptic copies 4th c.
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: Nag Hammadi codices, Coptic Museum, Cairo / related witness]
entries.summary:
A page representing the *Apocryphon (Secret Book) of John*, the central Sethian cosmogony preserved in several Coptic copies, including three in the Nag Hammadi codices.

## Iconography
The page is text, not picture: the *Apocryphon* is a revelation-dialogue in which the risen Christ instructs the apostle John. Its 'imagery' is verbal — the unfolding of the invisible Spirit, the Barbelo, and the aeons into a full divine world.

## Significance
The *Apocryphon of John* is the most complete account of the [[motif:sophia and the aeons|Sethian]] system: from the ineffable Monad emanate Barbelo and the aeons; [[motif:sophia and the aeons|Sophia]] acts without her consort and brings forth the misshapen [[motif:ialdabaoth|Ialdabaoth]], who steals power and fashions the lower world and its [[motif:archons|archons]]. It supplies the master-narrative of the demiurge and the entrapment of the divine spark — the spine of Gnostic mythology. Michael Waldstein and Frederik Wisse produced the synoptic edition.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'how the false world was made' lore-text: a single dialogue that gives you a creator-god, a flawed demiurge, his archon lieutenants, and a stolen spark to recover.

citations.text: Michael Waldstein & Frederik Wisse, *The Apocryphon of John* (1995).

entries.title: *Chrysopoeia of Cleopatra* — the Ouroboros and 'One is the All'
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Greek alchemical scribe)
entries.date: text c. 2nd–3rd c. CE; manuscript copies later
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: Greek alchemical corpus, e.g. Marciana/BnF manuscripts]
entries.summary:
The *Chrysopoeia (Gold-making) of Cleopatra*, a diagram from the Greek alchemical corpus whose central emblem is the Ouroboros inscribed *ἓν τὸ πᾶν*, 'One is the All'.

## Iconography
A serpent bites its own tail in a closed ring, the upper half dark and the lower light, encircling the motto *hen to pan*. Around it are the double-ringed device and shorthand symbols of the metals and the work. The whole compresses a cosmology into a single self-consuming circle.

## Significance
The [[motif:ouroboros|Ouroboros]] — the tail-eater that is simultaneously destroyed and reborn — became the master-symbol of alchemical and Gnostic unity: all things proceed from and return to the One. Ascribed to *Cleopatra the alchemist* and transmitted in the same late-antique Egyptian milieu that produced the magical gems and the Hermetica, the device links the [[motif:demiurge|cosmic serpent]] of the Ophites to the laboratory's promise of transmutation. It is a touchstone for Jung's *Psychology and Alchemy*.

## For artists & game designers
The single most reusable esoteric glyph: a half-light, half-dark self-eating serpent ring with a motto — instant emblem for cycles, rebirth, or a closed cosmos.

entries.title: *Sophia the Wisdom of God* — Novgorod palitsa (1654)
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Russian embroiderers)
entries.repository: Novgorod Museum of Art and History
entries.summary:
An embroidered *palitsa* (liturgical vestment-panel) of 1654 from Novgorod, depicting *Sophia, the Wisdom of God* enthroned as a fiery winged angel.

## Iconography
Sophia sits crowned and winged at the centre, her face and body fiery red, enthroned within a mandorla and flanked by the Virgin and John the Baptist with Christ above — the classic Novgorod 'Sophia' type rendered in gold and silk thread. The medium is needlework, the figures stitched in metallic and coloured threads.

## Significance
In Eastern Christianity Holy Wisdom is personified as a regal fiery angel, an iconographic type that preserves, in orthodox form, the Gnostic intuition of [[motif:sophia and the aeons|Sophia]] as a divine feminine hypostasis between God and creation. The later Russian *sophiology* of Solovyov and Bulgakov drew directly on such images. That this example is an embroidered vestment shows the type migrating from panel painting into the liturgy itself.

## For artists & game designers
A luminous patron-deity portrait in an unusual medium — gold-thread embroidery — for a 'Wisdom' faction banner or vestment.

citations.text: Sergius Bulgakov, *Sophia, the Wisdom of God* (1937).

entries.title: *Saint Sophia, the Wisdom of God* — Russian icon
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Russian icon painter)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: Russian collection, via Google Art Project]
entries.summary:
A painted Russian icon of *Saint Sophia, the Wisdom of God*, showing Divine Wisdom as a crowned fiery-faced angel enthroned amid an angelic court.

## Iconography
The winged, fire-coloured Sophia is enthroned within a radiant mandorla, flanked by the Virgin and John the Baptist with the blessing Christ above — a deesis arrangement around personified Wisdom. The throne is set on pillars, evoking Proverbs 9:1, 'Wisdom hath builded her house, she hath hewn out her seven pillars'.

## Significance
Where the Novgorod embroidery renders the type in thread, this painted icon gives its standard panel form. The 'Sophia, Wisdom of God' iconography keeps alive within Orthodoxy the figure of Wisdom as a divine hypostasis — the same [[motif:sophia and the aeons|Aeon Sophia]] whose fall, in Gnostic myth, sets the cosmos in motion. The two images together document how a heterodox idea was domesticated into orthodox devotion.

## For artists & game designers
A second, painterly take on the 'fiery Wisdom' deity, useful as a higher-fidelity portrait alongside the embroidered version.

entries.title: The Cathars expelled from Carcassonne (1209)
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Workshop of the Master of Boucicaut
entries.summary:
An early-fifteenth-century illuminated miniature, from the workshop of the Master of Boucicaut, showing the Cathars being expelled from Carcassonne in 1209 during the Albigensian Crusade.

## Iconography
A file of stripped, dispossessed townspeople streams out through the gate of a turreted city as armoured figures look on. The miniature compresses the violent suppression of the Languedoc heresy into a single procession of the expelled.

## Significance
The Cathars ('the pure') were a medieval [[motif:dualist heresy|dualist]] movement who held the visible world to be the work of an evil creator and salvation to lie in the spirit's escape from matter — a structure that scholars have long compared to ancient Gnosticism. Their *perfecti* renounced the flesh; their challenge to the Church provoked the Albigensian Crusade (1209–1229) and the Inquisition. Whether Catharism was a true survival of Gnosticism or a fresh dualism is debated (Mark Pegg vs. the older 'great heresy' thesis), but its imagery is the medieval face of the [[motif:demiurge|evil-creator]] idea.

## For artists & game designers
A grim event-illustration: a heretic people driven from a walled city — usable for any 'purge of the dissenters' story beat.

entries.title: Château de Montségur — the last Cathar stronghold
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Chatsam (photographer)
entries.summary:
The ruined Château de Montségur on its rocky *pog* in the Ariège — the fortress that became the last redoubt and the funeral pyre of the Cathar church.

## Iconography
Weathered stone curtain-walls crown a sheer mountain summit above the Pyrenean foothills. The surviving castle is mostly a later rebuilding on the Cathar-era site, but its silhouette has become the emblem of the lost dualist faith.

## Significance
Montségur fell in March 1244 after a ten-month siege; some two hundred Cathar *perfecti* who refused to recant were burned at the foot of the mountain, on the spot still called the *Prat dels Cremats* ('field of the burned'). The end of Montségur is conventionally taken as the death of organised Catharism and the close of the medieval [[motif:dualist heresy|dualist]] resistance to Rome. Its romance has since attracted endless legend, from the 'Cathar treasure' to spurious Grail myths.

## For artists & game designers
A real last-stand location: an impregnable-looking ruin on a knife-edge peak, the archetypal mountaintop fortress of a doomed faith.

entries.title: Bosnian Bogomil tombstone (*stećak*), reproduced 1901
entries.work: Gnostic Scripture & Cosmology (Codices, Demiurge & Dualist Heresy)
entries.creator: Hugo Charlemont (drawing), after a medieval stećak
entries.repository: in situ, Bosnia (reproduced in a 1901 publication)
entries.summary:
A 1901 reproduction (after a drawing by Hugo Charlemont) of a medieval Bosnian *stećak* — a monumental carved tombstone traditionally associated with the Bogomils.

## Iconography
A massive recumbent or standing stone block bears carved relief: arcading, rosettes, hunting and dance scenes, and emblematic figures in the distinctive idiom of the *stećci*. The carving is bold and flat, more emblem than portrait.

## Significance
The Bogomils were a tenth-century Balkan [[motif:dualist heresy|dualist]] movement — named for the priest Bogomil — who, like the later [[motif:cathars|Cathars]] they helped inspire, rejected the material creation as the devil's work. Older scholarship tied the spectacular *stećci* to the heretical 'Bosnian Church'; more recent work questions any neat Bogomil attribution, treating the tombs as a broader regional culture. Either way they are the most monumental surviving trace of medieval Balkan dualism, and a key link in the chain from the Paulicians to the Cathars.

## For artists & game designers
A carved-megalith asset for a heretic or 'old faith' graveyard: flat-relief tombstones with cryptic dancers and rosettes.
